examples.title: Warshall's Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to compute transitive closure of a directed graph
examples.code:
WARSHALL(G)
// G is adjacency matrix with n vertices
BEGIN
 R ← G // Initialize reachability matrix
 
 FOR k ← 1 TO n DO
 FOR i ← 1 TO n DO
 FOR j ← 1 TO n DO
 R[i][j] ← R[i][j] OR (R[i][k] AND R[k][j])
 END FOR
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 RETURN R // Transitive closure matrix
END
examples.explanation: The algorithm starts with the adjacency matrix. For each intermediate vertex k, it checks if a path exists from i to j through k. If yes, it marks R[i][j] as 1 (true). The OR operation keeps existing paths, AND checks new paths through k.

examples.title: Floyd's Algorithm
examples.description: Algorithm to compute all-pairs shortest paths in a weighted graph
examples.code:
FLOYD(G)
// G is adjacency matrix with edge weights, n vertices
BEGIN
 // Initialize distance matrix D
 FOR i ← 1 TO n DO
 FOR j ← 1 TO n DO
 IF i = j THEN
 D[i][j] ← 0
 ELSE IF edge exists i→j THEN
 D[i][j] ← weight(i,j)
 ELSE
 D[i][j] ← ∞
 END IF
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 // Main algorithm - consider each vertex as intermediate
 FOR k ← 1 TO n DO
 FOR i ← 1 TO n DO
 FOR j ← 1 TO n DO
 D[i][j] ← MIN(D[i][j], D[i][k] + D[k][j])
 END FOR
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 RETURN D // Shortest path distances matrix
END
examples.explanation: First initializes the distance matrix with direct edge weights. Then iteratively considers each vertex k as a potential intermediate point and updates distances if going through k gives a shorter path. The MIN function chooses the shorter of direct path or path via k.

examples.title: Floyd's Algorithm with Path Reconstruction
examples.description: Extended Floyd's algorithm to reconstruct shortest paths
examples.code:
FLOYD_WITH_PATH(G)
BEGIN
 // Initialize D and P (predecessor matrix)
 FOR i ← 1 TO n DO
 FOR j ← 1 TO n DO
 D[i][j] ← weight(i,j) or ∞
 P[i][j] ← NIL
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 FOR k ← 1 TO n DO
 FOR i ← 1 TO n DO
 FOR j ← 1 TO n DO
 IF D[i][k] + D[k][j] < D[i][j] THEN
 D[i][j] ← D[i][k] + D[k][j]
 P[i][j] ← P[k][j] // Store predecessor
 END IF
 END FOR
 END FOR
 END FOR
 
 RETURN D, P
END

PATH(i, j, P)
BEGIN
 IF P[i][j] = NIL THEN
 RETURN []
 ELSE
 RETURN PATH(i, P[i][j], P) + [j]
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: The predecessor matrix P stores the intermediate vertex on the shortest path. When a shorter path is found through k, we update both distance and predecessor. PATH function recursively reconstructs the complete path.
